industries.name: Phosphors & Display Technology
uses: Europium(III) red phosphor (Y₂O₃:Eu³⁺, YVO₄:Eu³⁺): the essential red component in fluorescent lamp tricolor phosphor blends and CRT color television — Eu³⁺ f-f emission at 611nm and 626nm produces the saturated red impossible to achieve with other phosphor systems

Europium(II) blue phosphor (BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu²⁺, BAM): the blue component in fluorescent tricolor lamps — Eu²⁺ d-f broadband emission at 450nm provides the cool blue component alongside green (Tb) and red (Eu) for white light
LED phosphors: Eu²⁺-doped nitride phosphors (Sr₂Si₅N₈:Eu²⁺, CaAlSiN₃:Eu²⁺) produce red emission for warm-white and high-color-rendering LED lighting — essential for achieving CRI>90 in LED bulbs
Plasma display panel (PDP) phosphors used Eu³⁺ red and Eu²⁺ blue for the highest-quality color rendering in large-format displays — largely obsolete as OLED and LCD have displaced plasma
Quantum dot displays: Eu-doped quantum dots explored for next-generation narrow-band red emitters in display backlighting applications

companies.name: Nichia Corporation
companies.use: World's largest LED manufacturer and major phosphor developer. Produces Eu²⁺-doped nitride and oxide phosphors for warm-white and high-CRI LED lighting applications globally.

companies.name: Merck KGaA (Performance Materials)
companies.use: Major rare earth phosphor manufacturer producing Eu³⁺ red, Eu²⁺ blue, and Tb³⁺ green phosphors for fluorescent lamps and specialty display applications under the Lumilux brand.

industries.name: Security Printing & Anti-Counterfeiting
uses: Europium luminescent inks for banknote security features — Eu³⁺-doped compounds fluoresce bright red under UV (365nm) illumination. Euro banknotes, US dollars, and most world currencies incorporate Eu-based security features invisible to the naked eye
Europium-doped glass fibers woven into paper currency substrate — fiber fluorescence patterns under UV light are difficult to replicate without the genuine rare earth phosphor
Product authentication labels: Eu-containing security pigments applied to pharmaceutical packaging, luxury goods, and official documents to verify authenticity with UV inspection devices

Europium chelate fluorescent labels in time-resolved fluorescence immunoassay (TRFIA) — Eu³⁺ chelates have millisecond fluorescence lifetimes enabling background-free detection in biological assays by time-gating

companies.name: De La Rue
companies.use: World's largest commercial banknote printer. Incorporates europium-based security phosphors and luminescent features into banknotes for over 140 central banks, making it one of the world's largest consumers of security-grade europium compounds.

companies.name: Giesecke+Devrient (G+D)
companies.use: Major banknote and secure document manufacturer using europium luminescent inks and fibers in currency security features. Also produces identity documents, passports, and chip-based payment cards with europium authentication features.

industries.name: Medical Diagnostics & Research
uses: Europium chelate labels in DELFIA (dissociation-enhanced lanthanide fluorescence immunoassay) technology — the most sensitive immunoassay platform for many clinical analytes including troponin, PSA, and thyroid hormones

Time-resolved fluorescence (TRF) using Eu³⁺ chelates for high-throughput screening of drug candidates — long fluorescence lifetime (>1ms) eliminates autofluorescence background from biological samples
Europium-labeled antibodies and DNA probes for fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) and gene expression assays in molecular diagnostics

Europium-doped upconverting nanoparticles for bioimaging and point-of-care lateral flow assays — visible emission when excited by near-infrared light eliminates autofluorescence from biological tissues

companies.name: PerkinElmer
companies.use: Dominant supplier of DELFIA europium chelate assay reagents for clinical immunoassay and pharmaceutical drug screening. Its LANCE and DELFIA platforms using Eu³⁺ cryptate labels are the industry standard for TR-FRET and TRF assays.
